key_content.drugs_data_model: Full schema for DrugsMS (MS-SQL): Drugs (22 cols), DrugCode (30+ cols incl HCPCS/NDC/RxCUI/BillingUnit), AdditionalDrugCode (GPI/NDC/strength/route), DrugFormularyData (PA/StepTherapy/Tier/Preferred/QuantityLimits per payer+LOB). Version 44 — most heavily edited page.
key_content.case_intake_eligibility: Comprehensive workflow spec: 7 epics (Intake→Entry→Assignment→Processing→Finalization→Outbound→Reporting). Confirms scope validation, carve-out handling, dual eligibility, SoC program, network checks. 15 open design decisions.
key_content.rules_engine: Prototyping complete. Supports workflow logic + granular validation. Async operations needed (eligibility searches, payer APIs). Combinatorial explosion concern (LOB × client × employer group hierarchy). Event-based architecture with case rollback.
key_content.member_eligibility_sync: Case-centric 'Manila folder' model. Write-only schemas with version linking. Person identifier strategy (use when available, GUID when not). DSNP splitting. Drug MS stays single source of truth with separate NewUM config tables.
key_content.testing_strategy: Version 24 — heavily iterated. NUnit (BE), Vitest+RTL (FE), Playwright (E2E).
key_content.assumptions: Workflow steps are NOT configurable per client. SoC is payer opt-in program. Determination states configurable per health plan + LOB. Default configs exist but not for all fields (e.g., member ID format).

medallion_architecture.bronze: Raw ingestion; full-fidelity, append-only. Schema valid, no transforms, PII tokenized at write.

medallion_architecture.quarantine: Rows failing DQ at Silver route to silver.quarantine.<entity_name> with failure metadata.

eligibility_rules_taxonomy.$comment: Comprehensive rules analysis from Eligibility Person/Member workshop chat (captured 2026-04-07). 12-section analysis of how payer taxonomy permeates the entire NewUM V1 system.

grain_hierarchy.resolution_rule: Lowest-level grain always wins. Engine walks from Product (leaf) upward to MajorLineOfBusiness.
grain_hierarchy.note: Grain ranking is configurable per payer — one payer might rank Employer Group above Product Line.

configs_vs_rules.configurations: Simple parameters — letter templates, Member ID formats, UI settings, TAT, booleans. Change infrequently.
configs_vs_rules.rules: IF-THEN conditional logic at runtime. Support branching, prioritization, chaining, nesting. Triggered at workflow steps & events.

rule_tiers.tier_1: Result required — failure = hard stop, case blocked
rule_tiers.tier_2: Result not required — failure shown to user, confirmation to proceed
rule_tiers.tier_3: Result not required — failure logged silently

scope_validation_chain.note: 6 sequential rules after eligibility = 'Eligible'. Out of scope R1 but fully specified in PRD.
rules: Rule 1: LOB Validation — member LOB active and delegated under Health Plan
Rule 2: Product Line / Product Offering — active and delegated under matched LOB
Rule 3: State of Plan Issue Restriction — member state in payer allowed set
Rule 4: Identifier-Based (CarrierID, AccountID, InsGroupID, BenefitPackage) — carve-out check
Rule 5: ASO Delegation — ASO excluded from delegation = Out of Scope
scope_validation_chain.fail_behavior: Any rule fail = Out of Scope, blocks case creation

payer_taxonomy_consumption_matrix.scope_validation: LOB → Product Line → Product Offering → Carrier/Account/Group/Package + ASO + Benefits (sequential chain)
payer_taxonomy_consumption_matrix.np_drug_rules: Major LOB + LOB + Product Line + Billing Method + Diagnosis Group (multi-attribute match at Product Line depth)
payer_taxonomy_consumption_matrix.tat: Payer + LOB + Delegation Level + Request Type + Billing Method

eligibility_rules_taxonomy.key_insight: PDL and scope rules key on Insurance Provider + Major LOB. NP rules go deeper into LOB + Product Line. The configurable grain hierarchy resolution (lowest wins) maps directly to traversing the business hierarchy from Product (leaf) upward to MajorLineOfBusiness.
eligibility_rules_taxonomy.eligibility_naming_action_item: Ask app devs about snake_case vs camelCase, keeping SQL case insensitivity in mind. Fields: RxCarrierId, RxAccountId, RxGroupId (confirmed as 'Cory strings').

case_schema_detail.$comment: Detailed Case schema structure from System Design Document. Expands on cases_domain section.

core_tables.purpose: Root record. Holds live workflow state: status, priority, disposition
Immutable field-level audit log across all mutable tables
Pending work queue for the rules engine. Records discrete blocking/non-blocking actions
Append-only log of all inbound and outbound communications (fax, letter, call)
Append-only log of documents (lab results, auth letters, referrals)
Medical drugs attached to a case (administered in a facility, billed under medical benefit)
Pharmacy drugs dispensed directly to a patient. Handled separately from medical drugs
Radiation therapies on a case, identified by CPT code. Mutable
Genetic/molecular tests requiring PA, identified by CPT code
Eligibility candidates surfaced during patient search, before case assignment

core.pattern: Mutable records; CaseHistory provides field-level audit

auxiliary_views.pattern: Flattened, soft-versioned lookups from Provider and Eligibility domains

auxiliary_snapshots.pattern: Versioned, append-only reference data from Drug/Radiation/GMT

audit_mechanisms: CaseHistory: old/new value per column — full field-level trail on all mutable tables
CreatedAt + DeletedAt on element tables — history of clinical elements added and removed
Snapshot versioning: Drugs, Radiation, GMT snapshots stored immutably with the case
Immutable CaseMessage / CaseDocument — communications and documents cannot be retroactively altered
CaseAction with ResolvedAt — full log of what blocked or triggered action on a case
case_schema_detail.auxiliary_records_note: Provider (HumanProvider, Place) and Eligibility records are flattened into the case schema to avoid spanning the full database during rules engine fact-loading.
case_schema_detail.databricks_integration: NewUM pushes operational records to Databricks Bronze on daily batch cadence via Data Team REST API. NewUM never reads from Databricks at query time for case processing.

provider_domain_detail.$comment: Provider domain details from System Design Document — expands on provider_model section.

payer_submitted_provider_data.description: Provider data that payers send in their files. Includes payer's own proprietary client provider ID, name, specialty, and address.
payer_submitted_provider_data.requirement: Must be stored verbatim and returned on authorization files — payers expect exact round-trip fidelity.
payer_submitted_provider_data.implication: Payer-submitted data is a separate concept from the master provider record

use_cases: Provider found in ETL data, used as-is — no modifications
Provider found in ETL data, but requires corrections — user overrides specific fields at case level without modifying source record
Provider not found — created from scratch (e.g. out-of-network provider)

data_exchange_gold_to_newum.$comment: Three options evaluated for moving Databricks Gold data into NewUM PostgreSQL. From System Design Document.

options.name: Lakebase Synced Tables
options.approach: Databricks-native managed sync from Delta/Iceberg into Lakebase-hosted PostgreSQL-compatible instance
options.cost: $600-$1000+/month base (always-on instance + storage DSUs at 10x ADLS rate)
options.dev_effort: Very low — UI wizard, no code. IaC gap with Terraform.
options.speed_15m_rows: Provisioned Snapshot: feasible with 2 CUs (~2-3h). Autoscaling Continuous: too slow (150 rows/s/CU).
options.ongoing_support: Very low ops burden. Non-additive schema changes require table recreation.
options.critical_limitation: Only works with Databricks-managed Lakebase Postgres — NOT the existing Azure Database for PostgreSQL.
key_limitations: Synced tables are read-only in Postgres — all DDL denied
No logical replication out of Lakebase
UC row filters/column masks bypassed via direct Postgres connection
FK constraints cannot be declared or enforced
Null bytes in Delta STRING columns cause UTF-8 failures
Max 20 synced tables per source table
Full refresh temporarily doubles logical size usage

options.name: ADLS Gen2 Staging + Airflow + PostgreSQL COPY
options.approach: Spark writes Parquet to ADLS, Airflow DAG on AKS orchestrates parallel download + COPY to PostgreSQL with staging table rename-swap
options.cost: ~$3/month compute + $1-$16/month storage (depends on retention). PostgreSQL and Airflow are existing shared costs.
options.dev_effort: Phase 1 (core pipeline): ~2 weeks. Phase 2 (per table onboarding): 1.5-5 days per table.
options.speed_15m_rows: Benchmarked: 237s (~4 min) locally, 80-125s expected on AKS. COPY at 85K rows/s.
options.schema_drift: Auto-detected at startup: new column = abort, removed column = warn (loads as NULL).
options.recommendation: Safe baseline if a production decision is needed in the near term.

data_exchange_gold_to_newum.note: Apache Iceberg (Option C/D) also under evaluation — see tickets/187526-adls-delta-reader/ and option-d-gold-push-jdbc/

data_exchange_newum_to_bronze.$comment: Two options evaluated for moving NewUM PostgreSQL data into Databricks Bronze. From System Design Document.

options.name: Lakeflow Connect
options.approach: Managed CDC via PostgreSQL logical replication (WAL) into Unity Catalog Delta tables
options.cost: Serverless DBUs + ADLS storage. No egress within same Azure region.
options.dev_effort: 1-2 days total including network validation. Requires wal_level=logical + replication user on NewUM PostgreSQL.
options.speed: Initial 1M row snapshot: 10-20 min. After initial load, incremental changes land within minutes (near real-time).
options.ongoing_support: Low but not zero — replication slot needs monitoring. WAL bloat risk if pipeline doesn't run.
limitations: Public Preview — subject to breaking changes
Replication slot must be manually dropped when pipeline deleted

options.name: Lakehouse Federation
options.approach: No data movement — NewUM PostgreSQL tables appear directly in Databricks catalog via JDBC. Read-only.
options.cost: Minimal — compute cost only for query duration. No storage, no egress within same region.
options.dev_effort: Few hours once connectivity is in place.
options.speed: 1M rows: 1-5 min depending on row width and predicate pushdown. Latency grows linearly with volume.
options.best_use: Fast connectivity validation step, on-demand reporting, exploratory phase before committing to Lakeflow Connect.
limitations: Not suitable for large scans or heavy transformations
If PostgreSQL is unavailable, all queries fail — no cached copy

operational_practices.$comment: Operational and engineering practices from System Design Document — CI/CD, testing, retention, monitoring, constraints.

schema_evolution.pattern: Expand-Contract: new columns added as nullable (no default) first. After all consumers migrate, NOT NULL constraints added and old columns dropped.
schema_evolution.bronze_schema: Captured in Avro Schema Registry. Compatible changes auto-applied via Auto Loader with mergeSchema=true. Breaking changes require migration scripts.

cicd.branching: Trunk-based development with short-lived feature branches. Main branch protected.
cicd.promotion: main → staging (automatic on merge) → prod (manual approval gate)

review_gates.elevated: 2 approvals for schema-breaking, Gold restructure, or security changes

automated_checks: Great Expectations dry-run (production data via Delta Sharing)
Schema diff posted as PR comment
cicd.deployment: PR merge → ADO pipeline + Airflow CI → tests → DAB deploy to Staging + smoke tests → manual approval → databricks bundle deploy --target prod → post-deploy smoke test
cicd.rollback: Delta Time Travel (30 days). Job definition rollback via prior Git-stored JSON snapshot. Hotfix branches bypass staging but require 2-engineer approval + same-day post-incident review.

airflow.principles: One DAG per logical pipeline. Composable DAGs linked via TriggerDagRunOperator or Datasets API. Owner tag + Slack channel on every DAG.
airflow.databricks_integration: DatabricksRunNowOperator with PAT or OAuth M2M. Run IDs persisted in XCom for audit.
airflow.monitoring: Metrics exported to Prometheus → Grafana. Dead-letter monitoring alerts on tasks stuck >15 min. DAG run history 90 days; logs to Azure Monitor.

operational_practices.data_contracts: Every ingestion source must have a documented, owner-confirmed contract before production onboarding. Formats: NewUM CDC = JSON event envelope (Git-versioned); DrugsMS = OpenAPI spec; Payer Eligibility = payer-specific flat file specs.

security_compliance.context: HIPAA-regulated healthcare prior-authorization. PHI tokenized at Bronze write layer.
security_compliance.baa_requirement: HIPAA BAA coverage is mandatory filter for all third-party tooling (Fivetran, Airbyte, etc.).
security_compliance.audit_trail: gold.compliance.case_audit_trail stored in ADLS Gen2 immutable storage (WORM), append-only Delta, retained 10 years.
security_compliance.phi_classification: PHI field classification (beyond SSN, DOB, name) requires data-classification review with Legal/Compliance before Silver schema finalized.

constraints: No analytic queries against operational databases — all analytics from Databricks Gold/Silver
Direct SQL/JDBC to NewUM operational DB is excluded as anti-pattern
Airflow is sole orchestration layer — ADF introduces redundant orchestration surface
HIPAA/BAA compliance is first-class filter for all third-party tooling
Event Hub schema changes must be backward-compatible for zero-downtime ingestion

decisions: Versioning: Hybrid SCD Type 2 — VersionCreatedAt + VersionEndAt replaces EtlStartAt/EtlEndAt. Two time dimensions: (a) version validity window, (b) coverage dates.
Soft delete: DeletedAt ≠ VersionEndAt. VersionEndAt = superseded by newer version. DeletedAt = retroactively voided by payer. Both fields can be set on same record.

open_questions: Manual eligibility records — where do they live? Nick: separate override table (ETL will overwrite, breaks insert-only, audit issues). Jack: same table with source flag. DEFERRED.

Business Grouping ↔ ETL mapping — THE BIGGEST UNRESOLVED QUESTION. How does ETL map variable client data into admin hierarchy? Each payer has different levels/fields/grouping. Nick's Silver uses crosswalk per client. Jack: 'bronze layer problem' — seeding is config, linking is hard.
Person ID grain — What business keys for dedup? Nick: payer-scoped (member ID + payer). Jack: system GUIDs. Nick: GUIDs change every file load without business key grain definition. DEFERRED.

arben_adls_postgres_demo.data_format: Parquet (not Delta/Iceberg — 'can also be Delta but for POC using Parquet')

architecture.step_1_fetch: Parallel ADLS fetch — 32 threads, 52 files in ~60s (vs ~500s sequential). Files loaded directly into server memory, no disk I/O or temp files.
architecture.step_2_batch: Process in batches of 500K records to cap memory usage regardless of table size.
architecture.step_3_staging: Stream batches into UNLOGGED staging tables in PostgreSQL. Unlogged tables skip WAL, faster writes. Invisible to application queries — completely isolated from live table.
architecture.step_4_validate: Row count validation against ADLS source before proceeding.
architecture.step_5_swap: Rename-swap (atomic, <1ms): rename live→archive, rename staging→live. If anything failed earlier (missing file, row mismatch, connection error), swap never executes.

performance.load: 15M records in 3 min at 85K records/sec using COPY command
performance.copy_vs_insert: COPY sends all data in one stream; INSERT sends one SQL statement per row. Millions of inserts = hours; COPY = minutes.

load_modes.append: Fastest — pure insert, no conflict checks
load_modes.replace: Bulk COPY to staging + atomic rename-swap (demo'd)

key_design_decisions: COPY command over INSERT — orders of magnitude faster for bulk loads
Unlogged tables for staging — skip WAL overhead, acceptable since staging is ephemeral
Rename-swap over TRUNCATE+INSERT — if insert fails mid-way, TRUNCATE leaves table empty; rename-swap is atomic, no partial state
Parallel ADLS fetch — saturate network bandwidth instead of waiting sequentially
Memory batching (500K) — prevents OOM on multi-million row tables
open_questions: ADLS auth method — how does the Python script authenticate to ADLS? Same SP credentials that blocked our Option B?
How does the script discover which Parquet files to fetch? (Delta log? Fixed path? Databricks writes to known partition?)
What about schema evolution — if Databricks adds/removes columns between runs?

eligibility_person_member_deep_analysis.$comment: Comprehensive rules & payer taxonomy analysis posted in the meeting chat — appears to be pre-meeting research/synthesis from Confluence V1 PRDs. 30KB of structured content covering all 12 rule domains.
sections_covered: 1. Grain Hierarchy & Inheritance (configurable ranking, lowest wins, effective dating, tie-breaker)
2. Configurations vs Rules (simple params vs IF-THEN logic, Tier 1/2/3 execution)
3. Member Identification & Eligibility Rules (search, coverage dates, RBAC)
4. Scope Validation Rules (6-rule sequential chain, out-of-scope for R1 but fully specified)
5. Payer Drug Configuration Rules (PDL lookup: InsProvider + MajorLOB, benefit type, crossover drugs)
6. Non-Preferred Drug Rules (deepest taxonomy touch: MajorLOB + LOB + ProductLine, mandatory vs voluntary)
7. Auto-Determination Rules (regimen auto-approve, ancillary, case-level, custom regimens always manual)
8. Case Routing & Queue Assignment Rules (30-min degradation strategy)
9. Post-Denial Pathway Rules (classification, routing, timeliness enforcement, integrity)
11. TAT Configuration Rules (Payer + LOB + Delegation + RequestType + BillingMethod)
eligibility_person_member_deep_analysis.dominant_patterns: PDL and scope rules key on InsuranceProvider + MajorLOB. NP rules go deeper into LOB + ProductLine. Configurable grain hierarchy (lowest wins) maps to traversing business hierarchy from leaf (Product) upward to MajorLOB.

provider_business_requirement_gaps.$comment: Gaps spotted after provider-domain business requirements delivery. Reported April 8, 2026. Confluence refs: Ordering and Servicing Provider Information PRD, Rules and configurations functional specification.
provider_business_requirement_gaps.source: Business requirements gap analysis email (April 8, 2026) — reviewed by product team
provider_business_requirement_gaps.product_team_comment: Office contact information should be treated as the case-level source for follow-up communication including letters/faxes. Potentially configurable — in some scenarios (expert review/non-delegated, true post-service cases) outreach to ordering provider may not be appropriate. Intent: capture most appropriate current contact details for that specific case, not treat it as source of truth for provider demographic data overall. Address should come from provider data.

gaps.title: No Office Contact Entity
gaps.nophi_status: CONFIRMED — Michal 4/9: case-level office contact, two records (ordering+treating), provider_type enum, NOT overwritten by ETL. Auditability TBD (Joseph/Jack).
gaps.what_is_missing: No entity exists to store case-level office contact information (Name, Phone, Fax, Email). These are manually entered per case per provider role — not derived from provider master data and must not update it.
gaps.scope: Required for Ordering Provider and Servicing/Treating Provider only. Place of Treatment fax is derived from provider master data (separate concept).

downstream_uses.name_phone: Provider outreach; clarification follow-up (calling provider office during case processing)
downstream_uses.fax: Fax/notification routing (outbound fax letters and PA notifications)
downstream_uses.email: Provider outreach; notification backup (when fax unavailable or as secondary channel)

gaps.configurability: Section is configurable (default ON). Can be disabled per payer or case context. Missing rows = section disabled, not a data error.
gaps.cases_that_fail: All standard PA cases where fax letter, outbound notification, or provider-office follow-up is required (when Office Contact is enabled).

fix.notes: Format validation (phone/fax/email) at application layer. No row = section disabled. Sibling to CaseProviderSelection. Neither belongs in provider master data.

gaps.confluence_ref: Ordering and Servicing Provider Information — FR + Data Dictionary (Ordering Provider + Servicing Provider sections)

gaps.title: Organization Affiliation Not Modelled as First-Class Search Result Unit
gaps.nophi_status: CONFIRMED — Michal 4/9: T1-T2 linkage acknowledged as 'not working in Mattis'. Users make independent selections for ordering, treating, place of service.
gaps.what_is_missing: Requirements define search result row as: Provider (Type 1) + Organization (affiliated practice) + Organization NPI (Type 2) + Organization TIN + Service Address. T1T2Link connects DoctorT1 to FacilityT2 but unclear if it produces the required denormalized queryable unit. Organization NPI and Organization TIN must be resolvable from a single provider selection.
gaps.why_it_matters: If a provider is affiliated with multiple organizations at different addresses (across different health plans), each affiliation must display as a distinct row. This is the core disambiguation mechanism for provider selection. Without this, the search UX cannot be built correctly.

fix.notes: Denormalizes DoctorT1 → T1T2Link → FacilityT2 → Location path

gaps.confluence_ref: Ordering and Servicing Provider Information — Consolidated Search Requirements + Data Dictionary

gaps.title: Place of Service (POS) Code Has No Home
gaps.nophi_status: CONFIRMED — Michal 4/9: payer-configurable, CMS 2-digit code, captured at case level. Routed to Kim Jewell (config team).
gaps.what_is_missing: POS code (CMS two-digit code, e.g. '11 - Office', '22 - Outpatient Hospital') is required case-level field, conditional on payer configuration. No entity or field in the provider schema captures POS codes.
gaps.why_it_matters: POS code drives auth/claims accuracy and is part of Level of Care section in V1. Multi-POS handling is still an open design question.
open_items: Medical vs pharmacy POS handling not closed
gaps.cases_that_fail: All payer configurations that require POS codes (e.g. BlueKC for Pharmacy). Cases without correct POS may produce mismatched authorizations.

fix.notes: POS values should come from a payer-configurable lookup, not a hardcoded global list.

gaps.confluence_ref: Ordering and Servicing Provider Information — Data Dictionary: POS Code, Level of Care section; Rules and configurations functional specification

gaps.title: Facility Type Has No Payer-Configurable Lookup
gaps.nophi_status: CONFIRMED — Michal 4/9: payer-specific configurable dropdown, NOT global list. Routed to config team (Pero, Keeley, Kim, Evan).
gaps.what_is_missing: FacilityT2 has a type field (CHECK constraint with values '1', '2'). V1 requires a payer-configurable Facility Type (e.g. Critical Access Hospital, Rural Health Clinic, Center of Excellence) that is distinct from the structural entity type (T1/T2). This is a regulatory/contractual classification, not a structural NPI type.
gaps.why_it_matters: V1 states: Facility Type is driven by payer contracts/regulatory rules, not a universal list. Facility Type data is often missing/inconsistent in provider files. Behavior should be configurable per payer. Current EntityType CHECK constraint only captures NPI entity type.

fix.notes: Store selected FacilityTypeId on case-level POT selection. Do not rely on provider file data as sole source of truth.

gaps.confluence_ref: Ordering and Servicing Provider Information — Data Dictionary: Facility Type; Rules and configurations functional specification

gaps.title: Specialty Representation Ambiguous for Backend Use
gaps.nophi_status: CONFIRMED — Michal 4/9: taxonomy/specialty IS needed, standardize on taxonomy, T1 only. NUCC fields to store TBD (Inna/Alison).

scope_clarification.applies_to: Type 1 NPI only (Ordering Provider, Servicing/Treating Provider)
scope_clarification.does_not_apply_to: Type 2 NPI (Place of Treatment / Facility) — no specialty or taxonomy field in V1 PRD

data_dictionary.ordering_provider_type1: Has Provider Specialty — backend value, derived, not user-editable, no UI display. Source: Provider Specialty table. Used for: provider key, claims/provider matching.
data_dictionary.place_of_treatment_type2: Fields: Facility, Facility NPI, Facility TIN, Facility Address, Fax, Facility Type, POS Code. NO specialty or taxonomy field present.

search_results.provider_search_type1: Name | Specialty | NPI | TIN | Org | Address — specialty explicitly included
search_results.facility_search_type2: Facility | NPI | TIN | Address — specialty explicitly absent

scope_clarification.implication: V1 PRD does not require taxonomy/specialty for Type 2 facilities. Schema does not need to support it for facilities in V1. If data team wants to store taxonomy for Type 2 as future-proofing, that requires a separate product decision — out of scope per current requirements.

gaps.what_is_missing: V1 requires specialty as a backend-only field for network determination and outbound files (no UI display) on Type 1 providers only. Schema has TaxonomyLink + TaxonomyReference (full NUCC taxonomy codes), but requirements reference 'Provider Specialty' as a single human-readable value (e.g. 'Medical Oncology', 'Hematology/Oncology') while taxonomy codes are more granular and structured differently.
gaps.why_it_matters: Specialty is part of the key (location + specialty + tax ID + NPI) that ties to a single provider ID used for correct network determination and claims payment. If specialty is only available as raw NUCC taxonomy code, consumer domains (Case, Rules Engine) must perform taxonomy → specialty mapping themselves, risking inconsistency.

fix.option_a: Add SpecialtyCode field to DoctorT1 derived from primary taxonomy code at ETL time
fix.option_b: Designate one TaxonomyLink row as primary specialty via IsPrimary BOOL and document NUCC taxonomy → human-readable specialty label mapping
fix.notes: Either approach resolves the ambiguity. Option (a) is simpler for consumers; option (b) preserves full taxonomy granularity. Applies to DoctorT1 only — FacilityT2 does not need specialty per V1 PRD.

gaps.confluence_ref: Ordering and Servicing Provider Information — Data Dictionary: Provider Specialty; open item resolution on specialty as network determination key
